title: GDPR Storage Limitation Automation Evidence
exclusions: Data retained under active legal holds; records with regulatory-mandated retention exceeding organisational policy; pseudonymised data retained under Art.89 research exemptions.
evidence: Storage limitation automation evidence showing automated deletion job execution logs, records processed, retention compliance rate, and exceptions with documented justification.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.25(2) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.25(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.49: GDPR Storage Limitation Automation Evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.49' running on index=backup, aws:cloudtrail (DeleteObject lifecycle), application deletion job logs (HEC), gdpr_retention_schedule.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.5(1)(e) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.5(1)(e) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.49: GDPR Storage Limitation Automation Evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.49' running on index=backup, aws:cloudtrail (DeleteObject lifecycle), application deletion job logs (HEC), gdpr_retention_schedule.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Insert a test record with retention class standard and a retention expiry date 45 days in the past with no legal hold or regulatory exemption; verify the automation evidence search identifies the over-retained record.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Insert a test record with retention class standard deleted by the automated deletion job on its scheduled expiry date; verify the evidence search confirms timely deletion with no overdue records.

app: Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876), Veeam App for Splunk (Splunkbase 7312)
spl:
| inputlookup gdpr_retention_schedule.csv
| join dataset_name [
    search index=app sourcetype="deletion_job" earliest=-7d
    | stats latest(job_status) as last_status latest(_time) as last_run by dataset_name
]
| eval overdue=if(last_status!="success" OR (now()-last_run)/86400 > scheduled_interval_days, 1, 0)
| where overdue=1
| table dataset_name, retention_days, scheduled_interval_days, last_status, last_run, overdue
description: Storage limitation requires defined retention and deletion. Beyond index retention (UC-22.1.4), this use case tracks automated purge jobs, API deletion endpoints, and backup rotation for personal-data datasets — proving deletion automation runs successfully.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes app, backup and sourcetypes deletion_job. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Export dataset-level retention policy to `gdpr_retention_schedule.csv`; (2) instrument deletion jobs with structured logs; (3) alert data owners on failed or missed runs; (4) correlate with object storage lifecycle events; (5) include metrics in DPO quarterly report. Indexes required: `index=app`, `index=backup`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=deletion_job`.
visualization: Table (failed purges), Single value (datasets overdue), Timeline (job runs), Bar chart (failures by system).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Retention holds from active litigation** — Legal holds that override automated deletion schedules are intentional and documented in the legal department's hold register.

2. **Archive migration in progress** — Data moving from primary to archive storage may temporarily exist in both locations during the migration window without constituting over-retention.

3. **Deletion job scheduling delays** — Automated deletion jobs running on weekly or monthly schedules may show data slightly beyond its retention period between job runs.

4. **Regulatory reporting retention** — Data retained specifically for regulatory reporting cycles (quarterly, annual) beyond the operational retention period is covered under legal obligation basis.

5. **Pseudonymised data retention** — Data that has been pseudonymised and retained for statistical purposes under Art.89 exemptions may appear as over-retained personal data when it is no longer directly identifiable.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `app` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_retention_schedule.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_retention_schedule.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.25(2)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.25(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.49: GDPR Storage Limitation Automation Evidence." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `app` under `deletion_job`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Export dataset-level retention policy to `gdpr_retention_schedule.csv`. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 2:** instrument deletion jobs with structured logs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert data owners on failed or missed runs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** correlate with object storage lifecycle events. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** include metrics in DPO quarterly report. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Storage Limitation Automation Evidence," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of GDPR control activity.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`app`, sourcetype=`deletion_job`) with a subsearch on `dataset_name` across the last 7 days. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `dataset_name` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.49: GDPR Storage Limitation Automation Evidence]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.49,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=app sourcetype=deletion_job earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `dataset_name`, `last_run`, `last_status`, `overdue`, `retention_days`, `scheduled_interval_days`. Run:

```spl
index=app sourcetype=deletion_job earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table dataset_name, last_run, last_status, overdue, retention_days, scheduled_interval_days
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.49: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`failed purges`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`datasets overdue`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Time trend (`job runs`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 4 — Distribution (`failures by system`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We storage limitation requires defined retention and deletion so we can show we meet our privacy duties when people ask for proof.
